items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: Why was the Spanish Armada defeated in 1588?
items.correct: A combination of English tactics and fireships, poor Spanish planning, and the 'Protestant wind' storms that wrecked the fleet on the return.
items.misconception: Drake defeated the Armada single-handedly in a great sea battle.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth I personally commanded the English fleet at sea.
items.why: Drake is mythologised. The English fleet harassed but did not destroy the Armada in battle — most Spanish ships were lost to storms around Scotland and Ireland.

items.q: How did Henry VII secure the Tudor dynasty after 1485?
items.correct: Through marriage to Elizabeth of York, ruthless control of the nobility (bonds and recognisances), financial discipline, and crushing Yorkist pretenders like Simnel and Warbeck.
items.misconception: Henry VII was universally popular because he ended the Wars of the Roses.
items.random_wrong: Henry VII was Richard III's nephew and inherited the throne legally.
items.why: Henry's claim was weak; he ruled by control, not consent. Students assume the wars 'ended' at Bosworth, missing how shaky early Tudor rule was.

items.q: Who served as Lord Protector for Edward VI from 1547?
items.why: It's Somerset (Seymour). “Northumberland (Dudley)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who reconciled England to Rome in November 1554?
items.why: It's Cardinal Pole. “Cranmer” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days did Lady Jane Grey reign in 1553?
items.why: The real figure is 9. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
